title: Prayer, Part 8: The Preface to the Lord's Prayer — Our Father in Heaven
speaker: Mr. Steve Thomas

outline.point: I. Why Use the Larger Catechism? (A Brief Defense)
subpoints: A. The Larger Catechism is not competing with Scripture — it is thoroughly based on it
B. Each answer in the catechism is accompanied by specific, contextually sound Scripture references
C. The catechism provides a systematic summary of what Scripture teaches on a given topic

outline.point: II. Who Can Call God 'Father' and What Does It Mean?
subpoints: A. God is described as a compassionate Father who pities those who fear him (Psalm 103:13-14)
B. God delights to give good gifts even more than earthly fathers (Luke 11:13)
C. Only believers can rightly call God Father — by adoption through Christ (Romans 8:12-16; John 14:6)

outline.point: III. 'Our' Father — Not a Nebulous Universal Father
subpoints: A. God is not an uninvolved deist creator — he is intimately involved and nothing can separate us from his love (Romans 8:31-39)
B. 'Our' signals corporate prayer: we pray with and for others (1 Timothy 2:1-2; Acts 12:5)
C. The word 'our' contains: our interest in God as adopted children, and our obligation to pray for one another

outline.point: IV. 'In Heaven' — God's Majesty Does Not Make Us Equals
subpoints: A. Isaiah and Nehemiah model the right attitude: reverence toward God who dwells in heaven
B. We approach with reverence but not timidity — Christ has opened access to the throne of grace
C. The dual ditches: flippancy on one side, paralytic timidity on the other

outline.point: V. Closing Exhortation: Be a Praying People
subpoints: A. The tools of this series are meant to be used, not shelved
C. Ephesians 6:18: praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit

summary:
In the final lesson of the prayer series, Mr. Steve Thomas addresses Westminster Larger Catechism question 189, which asks what the preface to the Lord's Prayer teaches. After a brief defense of using the catechism — showing that it is not a substitute for Scripture but a systematically organized summary of it — he unpacks three words in the preface: Father, our, and in heaven. On Father, he asks who can rightly call God their Father and what that means, drawing on Psalm 103, Luke 11, and Romans 8 to show that God is not a distant creator but an intimately involved Father who pities us, delights to give us gifts, and has adopted us through Christ.

On the word "our," Thomas refutes the idea of a nebulous universal fatherhood and instead shows that the plural points to two realities: the believer's personal adoption (our interest therein) and the call to pray corporately and for others (1 Timothy 2, Acts 12). On "in heaven," he describes the catechism's dual affirmation: we approach God with reverence because he is sovereign and holy, and yet with confidence because Christ has opened the way. He closes the entire series with a charge from Paul: do not put these tools on a shelf. We have learned how to pray correctly — now go forth as a praying people.
transcript:
## Opening

So we're going to finish up our series on prayer and we're going to be looking at the preface to the Lord's prayer today. Before we begin, let's pray. Father, thank you for the opportunity to meet today. Thank you for all the work that's gone into what we just heard. Pray that you would give the session and the deacons wisdom as they plan and pray and attempt to do things that are right for us and for your kingdom. And Father, pray that you would be with us as we learn today. Pray that you would help us to truly consider prayer and that we would grow in that discipline as a result of this series. In Jesus' name, Amen.

## Series Review

Like I said, we're going to start out with a review — actually it's going to be a longer review. Because we've had a week off with Christmas, I want to go a little bit more in depth in the review.

What is prayer? The Larger Catechism defines it as offering up our desires unto God in the name of Christ by the help of the Spirit, with confession of our sins and thankful acknowledgement of his mercy. A conversation with God where we talk about our desires, we use the name of Christ, the Spirit helps us, we confess our sins, we're thankful for his mercy as we pray.

To whom do we pray? To God the Father. Why? God only is able to search our hearts, hear requests, pardon sins, fulfill desires. He only is to be believed in and worshiped, and so we pray to him only.

How and why do we pray in the name of Christ? He's the mediator. When we pray in the name of Christ, we pray in obedience to his command and confidence of his promises. We ask for mercy for his sake. Not just mentioning his name as a talisman at the end of our prayer — it's a process where we draw encouragement from him to pray, and through his mediation our prayer is accepted.

How does the Spirit help us to pray? He helps us to know what to pray for, for whom, and how — and he helps us bring the right attitudes to prayer.

For whom do we pray? The whole church of God, all men, specifically magistrates, ministers, ourselves, our brethren, even our enemies, for all sorts of men living. Who do we not pray for? The dead and those known to have sinned the sin unto death.

For what do we pray? All things tending to the glory of God, for the welfare of the church, our own and others' good, but not for anything unlawful.

How do we pray? With an awful apprehension of the majesty of God, a deep sense of our own unworthiness, necessities and sins, with penitent, thankful, and enlarged hearts, with understanding, faith, sincerity, fervency, love and perseverance, waiting upon him with humble submission to his will.

How do we use the Lord's prayer? It's a template — but not just a template. It may also be used as a prayer in itself, so long as we're not using it as vain repetitions, but actually praying what's presented.

## Why Use the Larger Catechism?

Someone in our church asked me a couple of weeks ago why we're basing this series on the Larger Catechism rather than just using Scripture. I'll answer briefly. What I've learned is that the Larger Catechism is useful as a teaching aid because it's systematically organized and thoroughly based on Scripture. For the preface answer we're looking at today, there are no fewer than fifteen texts included as references. These texts are not taken out of context — they are applied in their right meaning, and they do fully support what the catechism says. We aren't choosing the catechism over Scripture; rather it's so scripturally based that it provides an excellent summation of what Scripture teaches on various topics.

## WLC Q189: What Does the Preface to the Lord's Prayer Teach?

Question 189 asks, what does the preface of the Lord's prayer teach us? The answer is: the preface of the Lord's prayer, contained in these words, "Our Father which art in heaven," teacheth us when we pray to draw near to God with confidence of his fatherly goodness and our interest therein, with reverence and all other childlike dispositions, heavenly affections, and due apprehensions of his sovereign power, majesty, and gracious condescension — as also to pray with and for others.

We're going to deal with this phrase in three parts: the word "our," the word "Father," and the two words "in heaven."

I want to start with Father rather than our, because it makes more sense to me to deal with it in that order.

## The Word 'Father': Who Can Call God Father, and What Does It Mean?

Two questions come to mind: who can call God their Father, and what do we mean when we call God our Father? These questions are necessarily related.

Psalm 103:13-14: as a Father pities his children, so the Lord pities those who fear him. For he knows our frame; he remembers that we are dust. And God deals with us kindly and in pity, taking all of that into account — unlike our earthly fathers, God never provokes us to wrath.

Luke 11:13: if you then being evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those that ask him? God delights to give good gifts, sending a greater gift than we would have dared to ask.

John 14:6: I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. Here we start to see the scope of who can rightly call God Father — not the unbeliever.

Romans 8:12-16: as many as are led by the Spirit of God, these are the sons of God. For you did not receive the spirit of bondage again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry out, Abba, Father. The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God — and if children, then heirs, heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ.

From these texts, we see that believers call God their Father by nature of their adoption. That's what we mean when we call God our Father — we reference his Father-love to us and his adoption of us.

A potential objection: by virtue of God's creation, aren't all men God's children? In Acts 17, Paul does actually refer to all men this way. But biblically this is the exception not the rule. John 8:42 and 1 John 3:10 show that some men are sons of God and some are sons of the devil. In John 8:42, Jesus explicitly says, if God were your Father, you would love me. The context of the Lord's prayer itself draws this dichotomy — Jesus is teaching his disciples, contrasting them with the hypocrites.

So: it means we run quickly to God as we would to an earthly Father. It means we trust God with a childlike trust. It means we love God as a perfect Father who will never sin against us. It also means we approach God with reverence, because he's our Father, not our equal.

## The Word 'Our': A Personal yet Corporate Address

Now the word "our." Three points: the distinction between "our Father" and a nebulous universal Father, why "our" and not "my," and the relation of private and corporate prayer.

Romans 8:31-39: if God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own son but delivered him up for us all — how shall he not with him also freely give us all things? Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Neither death nor life nor angels nor principalities nor powers nor things present nor things to come — nor any other created thing — is able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.

Psalm 125: those who trust in the Lord are like Mount Zion which cannot be moved. As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the Lord surrounds his people from this time forth and forever.

These passages together show us a Father who is intimately involved with our affairs, who cares deeply for us individually, and is not merely an uninvolved creator. He so involved that he sent his son to die for us, and nothing can separate us from his love in Christ.

Now, why "our" and not "my"? 1 Timothy 2:1-2: I exhort first of all that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks be made for all men, for kings and all who are in authority. Verse 8: I desire therefore that the men pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands. Even in Acts 12:5, we see the church praying corporately for Peter as he's in prison — and he's miraculously released. So as Paul exhorts us, we pray for all men, and we pray corporately.

So the word "our" contains: our personal adoption as children of God (our interest therein) and the call to pray with and for others.

## The Words 'In Heaven': Reverence without Timidity

While God is our Father, that does not make us equals. Isaiah 63:15-16: look down from heaven and see from your habitation, holy and glorious... doubtless you are our Father. Nehemiah 1:4-6: I prayed before the God of heaven. O great and awesome God, you who keep your covenant and mercy with those who love you and observe your commandments. Please let your ear be attentive and your eyes open that you may hear the prayer of your servant.

So: we approach God with reverence. We see God as just, holy, righteous, all-powerful, and all-knowing. But while that reverence requires us to have the right mindset, let's not fall into the trap of saying that God is so high we shouldn't bring our cares to him. Christ is our mediator and through him we have the right to enter the very throne room of God through prayer — and we are commanded to cast our cares upon him (Hebrews 4:16).

There are ditches on both sides. On one side: flippancy and unwarranted casualness — something carefully avoided in the prayers of Isaiah and Nehemiah. On the other side: timidity that prevents us from boldly approaching the throne of grace.

Secondly, we approach him with heavenly affections — as we pray, our desires, thoughts, and intentions are focused on God's glory, not on earthly things. And we're thankful as we pray because God is high and we are not, yet through Christ's sacrifice and intercession our prayers are heard.

## Closing Exhortation

As we wrap up, not just this lesson but our series, I want to offer a couple of closing thoughts. Over the past several weeks, we've had an opportunity to really examine what prayer is, how we pray, and why we pray in the way that we do.

Prayer is hard. It requires us to discipline our hearts and minds and even our schedules. That hardness can't be an excuse not to pray. It would be truly lamentable if we as a church, at the end of this series, said, "That's great, I can now pray a technically correct prayer," and then put that up on the shelf. We've got the tools now — how to pray correctly. So let's go forth and pray.

In 1 Thessalonians 5:16-18: rejoice always, pray without ceasing. In everything give thanks, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you. So we pray without ceasing.

And in Ephesians 6:18, closing up the passage where Paul's talking about the full armor of God: praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, being watchful to this end with all perseverance and supplication for all the saints.

People of God, pray.

Father, thank you for the opportunity that we've had to consider prayer, to consider the privilege that it is to come to you, to bring our desires, to bring our worries, to cast our cares upon you, to confess our sin, to offer thanks to you. Father, pray that you would mold us to be more like you as we pray. I pray that you would use our diligent prayers to shape us into the image of Christ. I pray that you would help us to grow in the grace of prayer. I pray that you would help us to desire to pray, to discipline ourselves, and to pray without ceasing as we watch and wait for Jesus' coming. In Jesus' name we pray. Amen.
